iterations.text:
The redistribution models uphold the uneven outcome we set out in Section III; they do so where the even account most needed them to fall short. RTI shows a slight rise in redistribution backing across all regimes (β=0.041, p<0.001). The spread across regimes that drives the exclusion finding does not show up on the solidarity side; the RTI × Liberal term is small and not meaningful (β=0.013, SE=0.019, p=0.488). Welfare setting clearly weakens the shift from threat into exclusion. It does not detectably weaken the shift from that same threat into redistributive solidarity. This is the uneven pattern. It is more than a missing finding; it is what the theory foresaw in Section III.F (P2) and what the even buffering account would have to deny.

The ISSP 2006 Role of Government module (N=10,216, 12 countries) gives us a second test on a different sample, outcome, and time span. We find no link between automation threat and welfare breadth on joblessness spending backing (β=+0.010, SE=0.016, p=0.55). This is a second null. The 2006 data come several years before the automation writings of Frey and Osborne (2017). On our reading, the shielding pathway needs automation threat to stand out as a clear threat to working groups in open talk before solidarity can be built around it in the political sphere. The harm pathway bears no such need for setting. The uneven pattern can be seen across time spans in which the underlying material threats are themselves shifting.

A reading that stresses measurement shortfall is open to us; the data let us make a sharper claim than the bare null alone would warrant. A two one sided t (TOST) sameness test against a smallest meaningful effect matched to the exclusion side moderation strength (|β|=0.06, the worth the even account would have foreseen) yields p_TOST = 0.008 for the ESS redistribution link and p_TOST = 0.001 for the ISSP added test (both at α=0.05). The 90% CIs fall wholly within ±0.05. The redistribution null does not merely fail to find an effect; it rules out a moderation of the strength the even account would foresee. A small shielding effect (|β|<0.03) stays within the reach of our data, but we must reject the even forecast.

The redistribution models bear out the asymmetric prediction from §III; they do so right where the symmetric account most needed them to come apart. RTI yields a slight uptick in redistribution support across all regimes (β=0.041, p<0.001). Here is the key finding. The cross-regime spread that drives the exclusion result simply does not show up on the solidarity side; the RTI × Liberal term is small and falls short of significance (β=0.013, SE=0.019, p=0.488). Welfare state settings cleanly dampen how hardship turns into exclusion. They do not, so far as we can tell, shape how that same hardship turns into backing for redistribution. That is the asymmetry, and it amounts to more than a missing link; it is what the theory foresaw in §III.F (P2), and what the symmetric buffering account would have had to overturn.

The ISSP 2006 Role of Government module (N=10,216, 12 countries) offers a second look, drawing on a fresh sample, outcome, and timeframe. The upshot: no interplay between automation exposure and welfare generosity on unemployment spending support (β=+0.010, SE=0.016, p=0.55). Another null. Worth noting, the 2006 data sit several years ahead of the automation talk sparked by Frey and Osborne (2017); the shielding pathway, as I read it, needs automation exposure to land as a felt, class-wide threat in public talk before solidarity can be gathered around it, while the harm pathway asks for nothing of the kind. The asymmetry holds across time windows in which the underlying material risks are themselves in flux.

One could read the redistribution null as a measurement snag. The data let us say something sharper than the bare null alone. A two-one-sided-t (TOST) equivalence test, set against a smallest-effect-size-of-interest matched to the exclusion-side moderation strength (|β|=0.06, the value the symmetric account would have foretold), yields p_TOST = 0.008 for the ESS redistribution interaction and p_TOST = 0.001 for the ISSP follow-up test (both at α=0.05). The 90% CIs sit wholly within ±0.05. So the redistribution null does not merely miss an effect; it outright rules out a moderation of the size the symmetric account would foresee. A small shielding effect (|β|<0.03) still falls within the data's reach, but the symmetric prediction is turned back.

The redistribution models bear out the lopsided forecast set forth in §III; and, notably, they do so precisely where the symmetric account most needed them to fall short. RTI yields a small uptick in support for redistribution across all regimes (β=0.041, p<0.001). Here is the key finding. The cross-regime spread that drives the exclusion result simply does not appear on the solidarity side: the RTI × Liberal term remains small and nowhere near significant (β=0.013, SE=0.019, p=0.488). Welfare contexts, then, cleanly blunt the shift from felt threat to outgroup hostility. They do not, so far as one can tell, do the same for the shift from that same threat into preferences for greater redistribution. That is the lopsidedness, and it is more than a gap in the findings; it is what the framework in §III.F (P2) foretold, and what any symmetric buffering account would need to contradict.

The ISSP 2006 Role of Government module (N=10,216, 12 countries) offers a second test, drawing on a fresh sample, a different outcome, and another period. The result: no interaction between automation threat and welfare generosity on support for unemployment spending (β=+0.010, SE=0.016, p=0.55). Another null, in short. Now, the 2006 data precede Frey and Osborne (2017) by years, before automation discourse entered the mainstream. On my reading, the shielding pathway hinges on workers perceiving automation as a shared, class-wide threat in broader discourse before solidarity can be mobilised politically; the harm pathway requires no such groundwork. The lopsidedness holds across periods in which the underlying hardships themselves keep shifting.

One could, of course, read the redistribution null as a measurement problem. The data permit a sharper statement than the bare null alone warrants. A two-one-sided-t (TOST) equivalence test, benchmarked against a smallest worthwhile effect matching the exclusion-side moderation magnitude (|β|=0.06, which the symmetric account would have predicted), yields p_TOST=0.008 for the ESS redistribution interaction and p_TOST=0.001 for the ISSP replication (both at α=0.05). The 90% CIs fall wholly within ±0.05. So: the redistribution null does not merely miss an effect. It rules out a moderation of the magnitude the symmetric account would require. A slight shielding effect (|β|<0.03) remains within what the data can detect, but the symmetric forecast is, plainly, refuted.

The models of support for sharing wealth bear out the lopsided guess from §III. They do so where we most needed the even-handed tale to fail. RTI foretells somewhat more backing for sharing across all regimes (β=0.041, p<0.001). We find no such swing across regimes on the side of fellowship. The cross-regime shift that drives the shutting-out finding does not show up here; the RTI × Liberal term is small and not meaningful (β=0.013, SE=0.019, p=0.488). The setting of welfare bodies cleanly weakens how weakness turns into shutting out. It does not, so far as we can tell, weaken how the same weakness turns into fellowship in sharing. That is the lopsidedness. It is more than a missing finding; it is what we foretold in §III.F (P2). The even-handed shielding tale would have to deny it.

The ISSP 2006 Role of Government unit (N=10,216, 12 lands) gives us a second test. A different sample; a different outcome; a different stretch of years. The upshot: no link between openness to machines and welfare breadth on backing for jobless spending (β=+0.010, SE=0.016, p=0.55). A second null. The 2006 data come years before Frey and Osborne (2017) spoke of machines taking work. We read the shielding pathway as needing openness to machines to stand out as a threat to one's kind in shared talk. Only then can fellowship be rallied around it. The harm pathway holds no such need. The lopsidedness shows up across stretches of time; the underlying bodily weaknesses themselves shift.

A reading of the sharing-null as a measuring hitch is open to us. The data lets us speak with more sharpness than the bare null itself. A TOST test of sameness against a smallest meaningful size matched to the shutting-out side (|β|=0.06; the worth the even-handed tale would have foretold) yields p_TOST = 0.008 for the ESS sharing link and p_TOST = 0.001 for the ISSP backup test (both at α=0.05). The 90% CIs lie wholly within ±0.05. The sharing-null does not merely fail to find a link; it rules out a steering of the size the even-handed tale would foretell. A small shielding link (|β|<0.03) stays within our reach to see; yet we can set aside the even-handed guess.
